answer:
**In Allama Iqbal's "Bang-E-Dara Part 2", chapter 17 ("Koshish-e-Natamam", translated as "Perpetual Striving"), the "unfinished effort" (koshish-e-natamam) refers to the perpetual, endless striving that animates and sustains all living things.**[^1]

This short poem (7 stanzas, 120 words) uses vivid natural metaphors to illustrate universal restlessness and yearning:
- Morning writhes in pain parting from the sun; twilight weeps blood for the evening star.[^2]
- Day burns like Qais for Layla (the evening); Morning Star yearns for endless light.[^3]
- Pole Star envies the motion of other stars.[^4]
- Springs seek rivers, rivers the sea; ocean waves ache for the moon.[^5]
- Eternal Beauty (nature) strains to unveil itself beyond flowers.[^6]

The closing revelation—ask Khizr (immortal guide) for life's secret—confirms: **"Through striving without end, all living things remain."**[^7] Iqbal portrays existence as dynamic motion toward an ever-receding goal, where completion would mean stasis or death.

[^1]: Allama Iqbal, *Bang-E-Dara Part 2*, ch. 17 "Koshish-e-Natamam" (/works/allama-iqbal-bang-e-dara-part-2-9bc58c/17-koshish-e-natamam/translation/).
[^2]: [/works/allama-iqbal-bang-e-dara-part-2-9bc58c/17-koshish-e-natamam/translation/#p-cbec4c]
[^3]: [/works/allama-iqbal-bang-e-dara-part-2-9bc58c/17-koshish-e-natamam/translation/#p-3d9a7e]
[^4]: [/works/allama-iqbal-bang-e-dara-part-2-9bc58c/17-koshish-e-natamam/translation/#p-576817]
[^5]: [/works/allama-iqbal-bang-e-dara-part-2-9bc58c/17-koshish-e-natamam/translation/#p-95469e]
[^6]: [/works/allama-iqbal-bang-e-dara-part-2-9bc58c/17-koshish-e-natamam/translation/#p-e19223]
[^7]: [/works/allama-iqbal-bang-e-dara-part-2-9bc58c/17-koshish-e-natamam/translation/#p-5cb383]
